title: New York Fed Releases Report Providing an In-Depth Look at Behavioral Risk Management in the Financial Services Industry

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York released a special issue of its Economic Policy Review on 'Behavioral Risk Management in the Financial Services Industry: The Role of Culture, Governance, and Financial Reporting.' The volume contains nine research papers examining corporate culture, governance frameworks, risk management practices, and financial reporting in banking institutions. The research was four years in the making and includes contributions from academics including Anjan Thakor, Andrew W. Lo, René M. Stulz, and Viral Acharya.
key_points: Release date: August 22, 2016 — approximately 9.5 years before today (2026-03-31), making this a historical record
Contains nine research papers organized into two parts: Culture and Risk Management (Part I) and Governance and Financial Reporting (Part II)
Paper topics include diagnosing/changing corporate culture, deferred cash compensation as stability tool, bank cash holdings, and board governance
Proposed mechanisms include deferred cash compensation to mitigate excessive risk-taking by employees and cash requirements to induce conservative risk approaches
Paper by Jonathan Macey and Maureen O'Hara addresses post-crisis bank corporate governance proposals
Research addresses increasing complexity of bank activities and governance challenges in holding company structures
why_it_matters: This research examines internal bank risk management and governance structures. While not directly about financial market structure (trading venues, market making, etc.), it addresses institutional risk culture that can affect systemic stability. The research is dated and does not reflect current regulatory frameworks or market conditions.
market_structure_context: This source focuses on corporate governance and behavioral risk management within financial institutions, not on financial market structure topics such as trading venues, market making, order flow, or market operations. The governance discussions relate to internal bank structure rather than inter-market structure.

issues_found: Topic mismatch: Source metadata TARGET is 'market structure' but source content addresses internal bank corporate governance, behavioral risk culture, and banking regulation—not financial market structure (trading venues, market making, order flow, market operations)
Source is a press release announcing a research publication; the actual research papers are not included, only summarized by title and brief description
No relevance to macro catalysts as defined: no rate decisions, data releases, speeches, or monetary policy actions
Age: published August 2016 (~9.5 years old), exceeds 90-day threshold but lacks clear archival value for market structure or macro catalyst research
Human review notes correctly identify this as borderline irrelevant to archive scope

human_review.notes: The record accurately describes the source content, but the source itself is not relevant to the archive's target topics of market structure (market microstructure, trading venues) or macro catalysts (rate decisions, data releases, speeches). Internal bank governance and behavioral culture research falls outside the specified scope.

notes: Source is a press release announcing a research publication from August 2016. While the New York Fed is a high-trust domain, the content does not address financial market structure (trading, market making, order flow, venue operations) or macro catalysts (rate decisions, data releases, speeches). The research concerns internal bank governance, corporate culture, and behavioral risk management. This is borderline relevant to financial stability but not to the specified target topics of market structure or macro catalysts. Marked as historical record due to publication date.
